Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty scattered this semester. I tried to dedicate about 18 hours a week, but it wasn't always consistent. I'm a visual learner, so I'd spend a lot of time creating diagrams and color-coded notes from the materials. Some weeks I'd be really focused, other weeks I barely touched anything because I was dealing with a lot emotionally. I found that working in bursts when I felt okay was more productive than forcing myself during low moments.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on how I was feeling and when deadlines were coming up. I wish I could say I had a regular schedule, but the truth is some weeks I'd cram everything into a couple of intense sessions. When my stress was really high, I'd avoid studying for days and then have to catch up all at once. Deadlines definitely drove when I studied more than any personal routine I tried to establish.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline looming or a notification about something new being posted. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if there was anything I'd missed, especially since my attendance wasn't great and I worried about falling behind. The peer support group I connected with would also remind me to check certain things, which helped. If I'm being honest, anxiety about missing something important was often what pushed me to log in.
Q4: There were brief periods where logging in felt more automatic, usually when I was in a better headspace. During those times, I'd check the LMS in the morning with my coffee, almost like checking social media. But that routine never lasted long—something would happen, my mood would dip, and suddenly weeks would go by with minimal logins. The habit just never fully stuck for me this semester.
Q5: The video lectures were incredibly helpful because I could pause, rewind, and really absorb the visual content at my own pace. I watched over 150 minutes of video content because I could study them when I actually felt capable of focusing. The discussion posts were also useful because connecting with peers made me feel less alone in struggling. When classmates explained concepts differently, it often clicked better than the original materials.
Q6: I tended to ignore the reading-heavy resources and some of the supplementary PDFs. They felt overwhelming when I was already stressed, and walls of text don't work well for how I learn. I'd tell myself I'd get to them later, but later rarely came. Some optional quizzes I also skipped because they felt like extra pressure rather than helpful practice.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see immediate relevance to assignments or exams, or when it helped me actually understand something I was confused about. If I could visualize the connection between what I was doing and my grade, I'd engage with it. Activities that felt like busywork or didn't have clear outcomes just added to my stress without feeling productive.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford when I was barely keeping up with required ones. There was always this guilt about not doing them, but required work had to come first. When I did engage with optional content, it was usually because a peer recommended it or I had a rare moment of feeling caught up. Required activities carried weight and consequences; optional ones felt like nice-to-haves that my mental state couldn't accommodate.
Q9: Absolutely, a huge amount of my studying happened offline. I'd download the lecture slides and videos, then work with them on my tablet where I could annotate and highlight. I also used external YouTube videos to supplement concepts I didn't understand. My handwritten notes and visual diagrams were probably where most of my actual learning happened, none of which the LMS would ever capture.
Q10: There were many nights where I'd be at my desk for hours working through problems using downloaded materials and my own notes. I'd have the textbook open, my diagrams spread out, maybe a Khan Academy video playing. The LMS would show nothing during those times. My assignment scores being so high compared to my engagement metrics probably reflects this—the work was happening, just not online.
Q11: Yes, constantly. I'd download a video or PDF once, and then that file lived on my laptop for weeks of repeated studying. I preferred working offline because it felt less overwhelming than being in the LMS where I could see everything I hadn't done. One download could turn into hours of study across multiple sessions, but it would only register as a single access.
Q12: Mid-semester was really hard for me. I was dealing with a lot of sadness and my stress levels were through the roof. There were weeks where just getting out of bed felt like an achievement, let alone logging into the LMS. My attendance dropped significantly during those periods because showing up—physically or virtually—felt impossible. The peer support intervention actually helped pull me back from completely disengaging.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted or overwhelmed, the LMS felt like this mountain I couldn't climb. I'd open it, see all the tasks and deadlines, and just close it again because it was too much. Competing responsibilities weren't really my issue—it was more internal. The fatigue from managing high stress and moderate anxiety meant my LMS usage was very inconsistent even when I had the time.
Q14: There were times I'd scroll through materials without really absorbing anything. I'd watch a video but realize at the end I hadn't processed a single thing because my mind was elsewhere. Sometimes I'd click through readings just to feel like I was doing something, but I wasn't really learning. Those sessions felt hollow, like going through motions without any real engagement.
Q15: I postponed discussion posts and some assignments even when I knew they mattered. The anxiety made starting things feel impossible—I'd worry about not doing them perfectly, so I'd avoid them entirely. It's a frustrating cycle because the avoidance made my stress worse. I knew logically that starting would help, but emotionally I couldn't get myself there until the deadline forced my hand.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the engine that drove my LMS usage. I'd have periods of almost no activity, then intense bursts right before something was due. It wasn't healthy, but it was how I survived the semester. The deadline pressure could cut through my avoidance in a way that nothing else could. My response time averaging over 16 hours probably reflects those last-minute pushes.
Q17: When I got good feedback on assignments, it genuinely motivated me to engage more. Seeing that my work was valued despite my struggles helped my mood significantly. But if feedback was delayed or felt generic, it didn't have much impact. The peer interactions and support I received were actually more influential than formal feedback in keeping me connected to the course.
Q18: The course having 13 different online components felt overwhelming at times. I appreciated the flexibility of online materials since my attendance was low, but the sheer volume made it hard to know where to focus. The structure worked for me when I was doing well, but during low periods, it felt like too many pieces to juggle. A clearer priority system would have helped me manage better.
Q19: The video content being available for download made engagement much easier for me as a visual learner. What made it harder was when materials were only accessible online or required multiple steps to reach. The discussion forums were designed well and actually helped me connect with peers who became part of my support system. But the overwhelming number of resources sometimes paralyzed me rather than helped.
Q20: I think that could reflect someone who's trying but struggling to convert effort into output. Maybe they're exploring, trying to understand, but can't quite get assignments together due to confusion or personal issues. It could also be someone who's anxious and keeps checking things without being able to commit to submissions. High activity doesn't always mean productive activity—sometimes it's just anxious clicking.
Q21: That actually resonates with my experience somewhat. My exam scores and assignment quality were decent despite low LMS engagement because I did substantial work offline. If someone understands the material through external resources or downloaded content, their LMS activity won't reflect their actual learning. Performance and platform activity aren't the same thing, especially for students who learn better independently.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show patterns of access—when someone logs in, what they click, time spent on pages. But they completely miss the quality of that engagement and all the learning that happens elsewhere. They can't capture whether someone was actually focused or just had the tab open. They definitely can't show emotional state, offline study, or the real depth of understanding.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks. It's when I'm curious, asking questions, making connections between concepts. For me, engagement also includes the emotional investment—feeling connected to the course community and believing the work matters. It's not about how many times I log in; it's about whether my mind is genuinely present and learning.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much the peer support intervention meant to me and how it improved my mood from a really dark place. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent with downloaded materials, creating visual study aids, or the emotional labor of pushing through high stress and sadness. My resilience in passing despite everything I was dealing with—that story isn't in the data. The numbers show a struggling student, but they don't show someone who fought hard to climb back up.
